title: Moulting and the autumn egg pause
subtitle: Why the eggs stop in October and how to support the flock through it.
excerpt: Every laying hen pauses in autumn while she grows new feathers. The pause looks alarming if you have not seen it before: scruffy birds, feathers all over the run, no eggs in the nest box for a month. A read-through of what is happening, what to feed, and when laying picks up again.
sourceNotes: Smallholder Magazine autumn poultry columns. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper, Internet Archive. AHDB layer-management factsheets covering moult and recovery.

glossaryTerms.definition: The new feather emerging from the skin, encased in a soft waxy sheath while it grows. Pinfeathers are tender and bleed if damaged; handle a moulting bird gently.

A gradual moult where the bird loses and replaces feathers a few at a time over six to eight weeks. Most modern hybrids moult this way. Heritage breeds often go through a hard moult, losing many feathers at once.

content.text: A first-time hen-keeper opening the nest box for the second week running in late October and finding nothing has often jumped to the worst conclusion. The flock is not sick and nothing has gone wrong. October is the moult. The hens are dropping their old worn feathers and growing new ones, and laying takes a back seat while the body's protein goes into feather growth. Eggs will be back by February.
Why hens moult

Feathers wear out. After ten months of laying through every weather a hen's feathers are tatty, the tips broken, the waterproofing weak. The autumn moult replaces them with a clean set that carries the bird through winter dry and warm. Day-length is the trigger; once the daylight drops below about twelve hours, hormonal signals shift the bird from egg production into feather regrowth. Pullets in their first autumn often skip the full moult because their feathers are still new; from their second autumn onward, expect a moult every year.
What it looks like
Modern hybrids usually go through a
soft moult

, dropping a few feathers at a time over six to eight weeks. The bird looks slightly scruffy but never bald, and laying may slow rather than stop. Heritage breeds (Light Sussex, Marans, Orpington) often go through a hard moult, dropping most of their feathers within a fortnight. The hard-moulting bird looks alarming: bare patches on the neck and back,
pinfeathers
emerging like porcupine quills, laying stopped altogether. Both shapes are normal. A bird in either pattern is healthy as long as she is bright, eating, and drinking.
When laying picks up again

Once new feathers are grown and day-length starts to lengthen in January, hormonal signals shift back. The first eggs of the new year usually arrive in late January or early February in southern England, two to three weeks later further north. Hens that came into lay the previous spring lay through their first winter; only their second-autumn moult takes them fully out of lay.
What to feed through the moult

Feathers are about ninety per cent protein. A moulting hen needs the protein in her feed to go into feather growth rather than eggs, and the standard sixteen-per-cent layers' pellet is on the lean side for that. Two adjustments help:

First, a small handful of a higher-protein supplement once a day for the eight weeks of the moult. Sunflower seeds at twenty per cent protein, hemp seed at twenty-five, mealworm at forty, or simply scrambled eggs (which the birds will accept without hesitation). A tablespoon per bird per day is enough.

Second, calcium is still needed even though the bird is not laying, because she is building new feather shafts and rebuilding bone reserves. Keep the oyster shell grit offered free choice. Do not cut back on the layers' pellets; the bird simply eats less of them through the moult.
Handling a moulting bird

Pinfeathers are tender and bleed if knocked or pulled. Avoid wing-clipping, deep examinations, or any handling during the moult that can be put off until the feathers are out. A blood feather knocked at the base bleeds heavily and is a vet job; better to leave the bird alone for the few weeks she is most vulnerable. Routine catching for the morning egg check is fine if done gently.
Lighting

Commercial layer flocks are kept in lay through winter by extending daylight with a coop light on a timer to fourteen or sixteen hours. A back-garden flock generally gets a clean rest instead. Birds that rest through one winter lay better the following spring and live longer overall. If you decide on a light, set the timer to come on in the early morning so the birds gradually wake to it rather than being hit with bright light at dusk. A red bulb dimmed to about ten watts is enough to suppress moult signals without disturbing roost behaviour.
What you might have found

attrs.heading: Moult worries
attrs.intro: A few patterns commonly puzzle first-year keepers.

items.symptom: One bird in the flock is in much heavier moult than the others.
items.cause: Some birds in any group go through a faster harder moult; breed and age both influence the pattern.
items.fix: Watch for general health signs. If the heavier-moulting bird is still bright, eating, and her crop fills through the day, she is fine. The other birds will catch up over the next week or two.

items.symptom: Bare patches on the back and shoulders only.
items.cause: Likely feather pecking from another bird in the flock, not a genuine moult.
items.fix: Watch the flock for ten minutes. If one bird is being chased and pecked, separate her with a friend in a hospital pen until the bare patches heal. Address the underlying boredom: more space, a hanging cabbage, scratch grain spread in the run.

items.symptom: Bird with bare belly and no new feathers coming through after a month.
items.cause: Could be a broody hen that has plucked her own breast feathers, not a moult.
items.fix: Check for sitting tight on the nest. Break a broody by lifting her off the nest several times a day or moving her to a wire-floored broody coop for three days.

items.symptom: Blood on a single feather that snapped near the base.
items.fix: If the bleeding is light, the bird will preen and stop it herself; pen her with friends and leave her alone. If the bleeding is heavy or continues for more than fifteen minutes, a vet visit is reasonable. Do not try to pull the broken pinfeather; it will heal in place.

content.text: The autumn pause is the simplest seasonal thing happening on the holding and the one most often misread. Feed for condition, leave the birds alone where possible, and look forward to the first February egg.
